content:
Key Findings

  • Wyre Council paid £326.5M to Lancashire County Council over eight years
  • The top 20 suppliers received a combined £630.8M from Wyre Council
  • £81.6M was paid to suppliers listed as UNKNOWN in the spending records
  • Balfour Beatty Civil Engineering Ltd received £39.2M from Wyre Council
  • Wyre Council paid £116.3M to the Department for Communities and Local Government

The Million-Pound Club

Wyre Council has paid out over £683.0M to 1,563 unique suppliers between 2017/18 and 2025/26. Among these, 20 suppliers received over £1M each, with the top 20 alone accounting for £630.8M of the total spending. This represents a significant portion of the council's overall expenditure.

The largest single payment went to Lancashire County Council, which received £326.5M. This substantial figure highlights the interdependence between local authorities and the shared services they provide. The second-largest payment of £116.3M went to the Department for Communities and Local Government, underscoring the council's reliance on central government funding and support.

Top Suppliers and Their Roles

The spending data reveals a diverse range of suppliers, from large engineering firms to local community organisations. Balfour Beatty Civil Engineering Ltd received £39.2M, likely for infrastructure projects and civil engineering services. Veolia ES (UK) Ltd was paid £16.7M, suggesting significant contracts for environmental services or waste management.

Local organisations also feature prominently. Fylde Coast YMCA received £3.5M, while Fleetwood Town Council was paid £1.6M. These payments reflect the council's investment in community services and local governance. The presence of Fleetwood Town Council and Fylde Coast YMCA in the top 20 suppliers highlights the importance of supporting local initiatives and community development.

Unknown Suppliers and Transparency

One notable entry in the spending data is £81.6M paid to suppliers listed as UNKNOWN. This significant amount raises questions about transparency and accountability in public spending. While it is possible that these payments are for services where the supplier's identity is not disclosed for confidentiality reasons, the lack of clear information can undermine public trust.

The presence of UNKNOWN suppliers in the top 20 highlights the need for greater transparency in how public funds are allocated. Councils should strive to provide as much detail as possible about their spending, including the identities of suppliers and the nature of the services provided. This transparency is crucial for ensuring accountability and building public confidence in the use of taxpayer money.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Wyre Council about the UNKNOWN suppliers and the nature of the services they provided
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about how Wyre Council allocates its budget and engages with suppliers
  • Check the council's published contracts register to explore the full details of contracts awarded to suppliers like Balfour Beatty Civil Engineering Ltd and Veolia ES (UK) Ltd
  • Write to your local councillor about the importance of transparency in public spending and the need for clear information about all suppliers
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and analyse how Wyre Council allocates its funds
This article is based on Wyre Council's published spending data covering 2017/18 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
